old_claim.note: Glassmaking provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct lighthouse_networks to glassmaking edge remains because the ancient Pharos-style scope is supported by tower-and-fire beacon evidence rather than glass optical systems.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica identifies the Pharos as a lighthouse on Pharos island in Alexandria harbor, finished about 280 BCE, with a fire burning at night and as the archetype for later lighthouses.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Britannica supports an ancient tower-and-fire lighthouse scope, not a glass-lens or glassmaking dependency.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Glassmaking becomes relevant to later optical systems, but the source-backed ancient lineage is pinned to elevated fires and harbor towers.

ontology_before: Lighthouse Networks depended directly on Glassmaking, implying ancient lighthouse systems required glass optical technology.
ontology_after: Lighthouse Networks is scoped to ancient tower-and-fire lighthouse systems and excludes later glass optical systems.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: lighthouse_networks no longer has a direct glassmaking prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to lens-equipped modern lighthouse networks.
A source shows the Pharos or early Greco-Roman lighthouses required glassmaking as a direct optical component.
A separate early_lighthouse_lens_optics node is added and intentionally depends on glassmaking.
short_reason: The ancient lighthouse scope is tower-and-fire based, not glass-optical.
